text: The robotic surgical setup includes four ports, with a ProGrasp instrument in the left lower quadrant. The surgeon uses a forced bipolar in the left hand and scissors in the right hand. The video shows an incarcerated right inguinal scrotal hernia, while the left side displays evidence of a prior repair with a three DMAX mesh, exhibiting clam shelling at the lower edge but no recurrent hernia.

The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap and dissects into the retrorectus space, identifying the inferior epigastric vessels as a landmark. The dissection progresses into the retropubic space, mindful of the previous laparoscopic TEP repair where mesh was placed in the retrorectus space.

The surgeon identifies the upper edge of the 3D Max mesh in the retrorectus space, adjacent to the inferior epigastric vessels. The peritoneum is taken down to access the pre-peritoneal space. The surgeon carefully dissects around the peritoneum, separating it from the testicular vessels using a third grasper for counter tension, enhancing precision in the dissection.

The surgeon uses the left hand to provide counter tension while dissecting the hernia sac, carefully pushing fat away to stay close to the peritoneum. The cecum, part of the sac wall, is visible, requiring cautious dissection. An avascular plane is identified, and the colon is seen forming the posterior wall of the sac.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to assist in dissection near the mesh, carefully navigating around the inferior epigastric vessels and vas deferens. The surgeon presses on the tissue with a grasper or scissors to distinguish between mesh, Cooper's ligament, bladder, and fat, making micro adjustments to safely dissect through the area. Upon encountering a large vessel after crossing the midline, the surgeon slows down to reassess the situation.

The surgeon identifies the bladder and continues dissection laterally in the space of Retzius, immediately behind Cooper's ligament. The lower edge of the mesh is visible, showing signs of curling. The surgeon carefully navigates around the mesh, ensuring the infrapigastric and external iliac vein remain on the opposite side of the mesh. The dissection is kept bloodless to distinguish tissue types, with fatty tissue indicating the absence of bladder in the immediate area.

The surgeon carefully works around the internal ring, freeing attachments of the hernia sac. The vas deferens is briefly visible as the surgeon navigates between the sac and surrounding tissue. Maintaining hemostasis, the surgeon identifies the tissue plane between the bladder and fat, and observes the pulsation of the external iliac artery. With assistance, external pressure is applied to the scrotum, aiding in the reduction of the hernia sac. The appendix is successfully extracted from the scrotum, allowing the surgeon to complete the dissection of the hernia sac.

The surgeon carefully identifies the vas deferens and ensures it is not divided while cautiously dissecting around the area. The sac is completely reduced, and hemostasis is maintained. The surgeon assesses the surgical field for any damage.

The surgeon aims for a seven to eight centimeter posterior mesh overlap by dissecting the hernia sac and right colon as far back as possible. Lymphatic fluid is visible. The surgeon connects the space of Bogros to the retropubic space, ensuring the mesh transitions smoothly from medial to lateral. The areolar plane between the fat near the femoral vessels and the fat near the bladder is clearly visible, indicating effective hemostasis.

The surgeon completes the dissection and assesses the old mesh, noting its curled appearance and attachment to the epigastric vessels. The mesh is loose in some areas, allowing for excision attempts to reduce mesh burden in the right groin. The surgeon carefully separates the mesh from surrounding fatty tissue, avoiding areas where the mesh is well-integrated near the femoral branch and genitofemoral nerve. Using electrocautery, the surgeon divides the polypropylene mesh, leaving a portion medial to the inferior epigastrics due to its firm attachment.

The surgeon excises approximately one-third of the mesh, which is positioned superficially on the exterior side of a core lipoma. The surrounding fatty tissue contains larger dilated veins, likely due to the inguinal scrotal hernia. The mesh placement appears unusual, contributing to the hernia recurrence.

The surgeon removes a fatty clump of tissue and observes the well-incorporated mesh left in place. They measure the surgical space to determine the appropriate size for a new mesh. The surgeon selects a large pro grip mesh, originally 20 by 15 centimeters, and trims it to 19 by 15 centimeters to ensure broad anterior-posterior coverage, avoiding the triangle of pain and triangle of doom.

The surgeon deploys a lightweight mesh, folding it like a taco and marking criss-crossing lines to identify the center of the indirect hernia defect. The mesh is secured in place, with no movement upon pressure. Excess mesh protruding above the flap is trimmed and removed alongside a piece of 3D Max mesh.

The surgeon cuts the large mesh into narrower strips, resembling an orange peel, to facilitate removal through the eight millimeter port. The mesh is successfully extracted, and the surgeon proceeds to close the peritoneal flap using a 3-0 V-Loc suture.

The surgeon identifies a sliding hernia posteriorly and observes a rim of hernia sac on the colon. Sutures are placed to close the visible defect, and the angiocath technique is used to ensure the colon remains on the correct side of the peritoneum. An additional V-Lock suture is prepared to close the remaining peritoneal defect.

The fellow inserts a fourteen gauge angiocath and removes the needle, allowing the pneumoperitoneum to push CO₂ out of the preperitoneal space through the angiocath, causing the space to collapse. The fellow then compresses the scrotum to expel CO₂, resulting in visible peritoneal vibration, clearly distinguishing the peritoneum from the mesh.
